Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.content: Let me check the animation to see if UP worked this time.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check animation for the RIGHT, RIGHT, UP sequence
anim = last_animation
for entry in anim:
    print("Action:", entry.action, "Frames:", len(entry.frames))
    for j, frame in enumerate(entry.frames):
        for n in frame.segmentation['nodes']:
            if n['color'] == 'b':
                b = n['boundary']
                r_min, c_min = b[0]
                r_max = max(p[0] for p in b)
                c_max = max(p[1] for p in b)
                print("  Frame %d: blue at [%d,%d]-[%d,%d]" % (j, r_min, c_min, r_max, c_max))

# Check final state
print("\nFinal state:")
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    r_min, c_min = b[0]
    r_max = max(p[0] for p in b)
    c_max = max(p[1] for p in b)
    print("  color=%s, pos=[%d,%d]-[%d,%d]" % (n['color'], r_min, c_min, r_max, c_max))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So UP worked again! The player moved from rows 16-19 to rows 12-15. So UP is NOT blocked - it works consistently. Earlier I thought it was blocked because the batched UP, UP, RIGHT didn't show movement for the UP actions in the animation, but that was because the animation only shows the final frame of each action, and the player was already at rows 12-15 before the batch.
Wait, no. Let me re-examine. After the first standalone UP, the player was at rows 12-15. Then I batched UP, UP, RIGHT. The animation showed:
- Action 0 (UP): blue at [12,16]-[15,35] (no change)
- Action 1 (UP): blue at [12,16]-[15,35] (no change)
- Action 2 (RIGHT): blue at [12,20]-[15,39]
So the UP actions in the batch DIDN'T move the player. But now, after RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, the player moved from [16,20]-[19,39] to [12,28]-[15,47].
Wait, that doesn't make sense. The player was at [16,20]-[19,39] before the batch. After RIGHT: [16,24]-[19,43]. After RIGHT again: [16,28]-[19,47]. After UP: [12,28]-[15,47]. So UP DID work here.
But earlier, when the player was at [12,16]-[15,35], UP didn't work. So the difference is:
- Player at rows 16-19: UP works (moves to rows 12-15)
- Player at rows 12-15: UP doesn't work (stays at rows 12-15)
This makes sense! The player can only move UP when it's at rows 16-19, and UP moves it to rows 12-15. Once at rows 12-15, UP is blocked (ceiling). Similarly, DOWN moves from rows 12-15 back to rows 16-19.
So the player can only exist at two vertical positions: rows 12-15 and rows 16-19. The target (magenta) is at rows 4-7, which is above both of these. So the player can NEVER reach the magenta target by moving UP!
This means the game mechanic must be different. Maybe I need to:
1. Move the player to a position where it overlaps with the target
2. Or use SPACE/MOUSE to activate something
3. Or the yellow platforms are movable and I need to use them
Wait, let me reconsider. The magenta is at rows 4-7, cols 36-39. The player at rows 12-15 can't reach there. But what if the goal isn't to move the player to the target, but to do something else?
Let me look at the yellow platforms more carefully. They're at:
- Left: rows 52-59, cols 16-27
- Right: rows 52-59, cols 40-51
What if I need to move the player DOWN to the yellow platforms? Let me try DOWN from the current position.
Actually wait - I've been assuming the blue bar is the player, but what if it's something else? What if the yellow shapes are the player? Let me try clicking on the yellow shapes.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at what happens when I move the player DOWN multiple times - maybe it can go below rows 16-19.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 12-15, cols 28-47
# Let me try DOWN multiple times to see how far down it can go
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
